briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. Welcome to the climate briefing for March 21, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Record-Breaking Heatwaves and Direct Warnings of the Climate Crisis
sections.content: International news reported today numerous accounts of record-breaking heatwaves, demonstrating that the climate crisis has become a reality. Despite being March, the Western and Southwestern regions of the United States saw existing records broken due to summer-like heat [11, 24, 26, 29], with one desert town in Arizona, in particular, recording 43.3℃, the highest March temperature in U.S. history [32]. Scientists point out that such heatwaves would be virtually impossible without the climate crisis [11, 19], and research from Peru linking record tropical disease outbreaks to extreme weather caused by climate change indicates the severe impact of the climate crisis on healthcare systems [23]. This aligns with domestic reports stating that the Earth has become hotter despite 10 years passing since the Paris Agreement, once again emphasizing the urgency of responding to climate change [2].

sections.title: ⚠️ Suffering of Vulnerable Populations and Policy Setbacks
sections.content: Domestic reports indicate that the climate crisis is disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations, particularly individuals with disabilities [1, 3]. The Pacific region is also noted to face 'unfinished business' for climate crisis survivors, alongside nuclear threats [31], suggesting the unequal impact of climate change on socially vulnerable groups. Despite this vulnerability, some policy decisions have contradicted the direction of climate change response. California approved numerous new highway lanes even amidst heatwaves [13], and Vermont saw a climate change education bill scrapped [17], leading to international criticism that legislative attempts to address climate change consistently falter in Washington D.C. [18]. Furthermore, Vermont and Wisconsin have even joined lawsuits alleging that the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has abandoned its authority to combat climate change [15, 21], raising growing concerns about policy reversals.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Efforts for Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to achieve carbon neutrality and expand renewable energy. Yuseong-gu conducted a river cleanup and carbon neutrality campaign in commemoration of 'World Water Day' [4], and HD Hyundai demonstrated its commitment to lead the carbon-neutral era by leveraging the energy crisis as an opportunity [5]. Muju Bandi Farm is accelerating digital forest management by providing training for forestry successors in connection with its 'Carbon-Neutral Garden' [6], and Ulsan Samjeong Elementary School took the lead in practicing carbon neutrality by planting saplings to commemorate Arbor Day [7]. Furthermore, the legalization of a renewable energy 'profit-sharing scheme' is being promoted, focusing on expanding public participation and acceptance [8]. Representative Jeong Hye-gyeong proposed the 'Korea Power Generation Corporation Act' to expand public renewable energy [9], and Siheung City is working to stabilize energy costs for citizens and businesses through the expansion of renewable energy [10], showcasing comprehensive efforts across various sectors.

sections.title: 🌍 International Response and the Importance of Education
sections.content: News that people concerned about climate change-induced chaos are finding answers in virtual classrooms underscores the importance of climate education [12]. Notably, educators in Eastern and Southern Africa are transforming climate change-related challenges into action [16], demonstrating how effective community-based climate action can be [20]. The role of the 'quiet workers' in the renewable energy transition is also noteworthy internationally [27], as they are essential in driving change. These examples illustrate that education and community participation, which raise awareness and encourage practical action on climate change, are crucial pillars in the global response to the climate crisis.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severe reality of the climate crisis, along with various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. This is a time that requires continuous attention and action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Arizona desert town breaks record for hottest March temperature in US history
articles.description: Martinez Lake, about 145 miles west of Phoenix, reached 110F (43.3C) on Thursday amid scorching south-west heatSign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inboxA small community in the Arizona desert has broken a record for the highest March temperature ever recorded in the US, as the south-west bakes in a blistering late-winter heatwave.The astonishing temperature was recorded just outside Martinez Lake, Arizona, which reached 110F (43.3C) on Thursday, according to t

summary.phenomenon: On Thursday (March), a temperature of 110F (43.3C) was recorded in a desert community near Martinez Lake, about 145 miles west of Phoenix, Arizona, breaking the record for the highest March temperature ever recorded in US history.
summary.cause: This record-breaking high temperature occurred amidst a fierce late-winter heatwave that struck the US Southwest.
